pick.rationale: Despite the devastating loss of Big 12 Player of the Year JT Toppin to a torn ACL and a three-game losing streak entering the tournament, Texas Tech's remaining roster still holds a substantial statistical edge over Akron, with a roughly 12-point adjusted efficiency margin advantage (KenPom +25.19 vs +13.02) driven by an elite offense ranked 9th-12th nationally. Akron's veteran-led squad won three consecutive MAC Tournament titles and brings a 29-5 record, but the Tournament Index grades them as one of the weaker 12-seeds in recent history, and their defense (99th-ranked AdjD of 105-106) will struggle to contain Texas Tech's Big 12-caliber scoring even without Toppin. The 8.5-point spread confirms the market still views Texas Tech as a comfortable favorite, and while the 5/12 seed line is historically upset-prone and Texas Tech's current form is concerning, the talent and efficiency gap is too wide for Akron to overcome absent a truly exceptional shooting performance.

reasoningStep.title: Texas Tech vs Akron – 5/12 Midwest Matchup Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Texas Tech advances despite significant injury concerns, as the efficiency gap remains too large for Akron to close even with the Red Raiders missing their best player.
evidence: Texas Tech ranks 12th-19th nationally (KenPom/Torvik) with AdjEM +25.19 vs Akron's 64th-68th with AdjEM +13.02—a 12-point gap
JT Toppin (Big 12 POY) is out for the season with a torn ACL suffered Feb 17; Texas Tech went 3-0 immediately after but then lost 3 straight
Christian Anderson cleared to play after groin injury in Big 12 Tournament
Texas Tech AdjO 125-126 (top 12 nationally) vastly exceeds Akron's AdjD of 105-106 (99th nationally)
Akron won 3 consecutive MAC Tournament titles but graded as 20th-strongest 12-seed out of 52 in last 13 tournaments
Spread is Texas Tech -8.5 with moneyline implying ~75% win probability for the Red Raiders
5/12 seed line historically produces upsets ~35% of the time, but this Akron team is a weak 12-seed by advanced metrics

Michael McDaniel , Kevin Sweeney | Share on Facebook Share on X Add us as a preferred source Texas Tech star JT Toppin will miss the remainder of the season with a torn ACL. | Aryanna Frank-Imagn Images In this story: Texas Tech Red Raiders Fresh off a 78–75 overtime road win against then-No. 1 Arizona over the weekend, No. 13 Texas Tech was upset 72–67 on the road by Arizona State on Tuesday night in Tempe, Ariz. --> Fresh off a 78–75 overtime road win against then-No. 1 Arizona over the weekend, No. 13 Texas Tech was upset 72–67 on the road by Arizona State on Tuesday night in Tempe, Ariz. The upset loss for the Red Raiders, which marked the Sun Devils’ first victory over a ranked opponent since January 2025, was one thing. But the bigger story coming out of the contest was the well-being of Red Raiders star JT Toppin, who fell awkwardly while driving to the basket with just over six minutes to play in the game. He limped to the locker room with assistance from the Red Raiders’ medical staff and did not return to the contest after scoring 20 points and adding eight rebounds. --> The upset loss for the Red Raiders, which marked the Sun Devils’ first victory over a ranked opponent since January 2025, was one thing. But the bigger story coming out of the contest was the well-being of Red Raiders star JT Toppin, who fell awkwardly while driving to the basket with just over six minutes to play in the game. He limped to the locker room with assistance from the Red Raiders’ medical staff and did not return to the contest after scoring 20 points and adding eight rebounds. Texas Tech shared the extent of Toppin’s injury on Wednesday afternoon, announcing that last year’s Big 12 Player of the Year will miss the remainder of the season with a torn ACL in his right knee. --> Texas Tech shared the extent of Toppin’s injury on Wednesday afternoon, announcing that last year’s Big 12 Player of the Year will miss the remainder of the season with a torn ACL

result.text: Is Texas Tech basketball's pre-March Madness losing skid troubling? Skip to main content Home Olympics NFL NCAAB NCAAW NBA Women's Sports NCAAF MLB WNBA Sports Betting Big Ten Sports SEC Sports Boxing Coaches Salaries Draft Hub Fantasy Football For The Win Formula 1 Golfweek High School Sports High School Sports Awards IndyCar MMA NASCAR National Signing Day NHL Soccer Sports Business Sports Pulse Sports Seriously Tennis WWE Youth Sports Outdoors Fantasy Bet With the People Clear Play Arizona Sports Indiana Sports Michigan Sports Ohio Sports Tennessee Sports Wisconsin Teams For Subscribers With 3 straight losses entering March Madness, is Texas Tech in trouble? Nathan Giese Lubbock Avalanche-Journal KANSAS CITY, Mo. — Nobody is going to pity Grant McCasland. He's handsomely paid to be the head coach of the Texas Tech basketball team at one of the most ambitious spending athletic departments in the country. But at the same time, you've gotta feel for the guy. McCasland has been the head coach at Tech for three full years now, spanning 103 total games . In that time, he's had his full arsenal available to him in just 16 games. Injuries have become second nature to McCasland that it seems he doesn't even flinch when another pops up. Half your rotation out for most of the year? Cool, let's set the program record for wins over Top 10 teams. Lose a national player of the year, Big 12 player of the year, and first team All-American candidate in one full swoop? Big whoop, we'll go into Ames and beat Iowa State. McCasland is an eternal optimist, always able to flip the negative into something deeper. It's part of what makes him such a great coach: finding joy in the struggle to reach the top of his profession. Even McCasland, though, has to think something's gotta give at some point. If it's not JT Toppin, having a historic season, tearing his ACL so close to the end of the season — after his team's bus tires were slashed after beating Arizona — it's seeing his other All-American, Christian Anderson, slipping and sliding all over the Big 12's insane ASP GlassFloor LED court , causing he and LeJuan Watts (perhaps an unrelated injury, perhaps related) to exit their Big 12 Tournament game with ailments. Compounding the current injury factor is the previous injuries to guys like Josiah Moseley, Luke Bamgboye and Marial Akuentok that set the team back from jump street. Before the season could even begin, the Red Raiders were shorthanded. It's the story of McCasland's existence at Texas Tech. Now is not the time to play the sympathy card, not that McCasland ever would. But he is a realist. As much as he wants to look at the bright side of things, he knows the Red Raiders and trending in the wrong direction and the worst possible time. Three straight losses to NCAA Tournament-bound teams isn't the end of the world. How they came about and what it says about his team's current state could be. The team has lacked poise under pressure, second-guessing every move th

result.text: Ticket Punched: Previewing the Akron Zips For the 2026 NCAA Tournament Articles Podcasts Scout Team Game Analysis Bracketology Women's Hoops Free Content Team Previews Scoreboard Search Sign Up Sign Up Create Your Account Get access to exclusive features and stay updated. Sign Up Sign Up Already have an account? Log In Articles Podcasts Scout Team Game Analysis Bracketology Women's Hoops Free Content Team Previews Scoreboard Become a member Become a member Home Articles & Editorials Ticket Punched: Previewing the Akron Zips For the 2026 NCAA Tournament Articles & Editorials Articles & Editorials Bracketology Bracketology Free Content Free Content Ticket Punched: Previewing the Akron Zips For the 2026 NCAA Tournament Featured Mar 15, 2026 by Brian Rauf No team in MAC history had ever won three conference tournaments in a row until Akron accomplished that feat on Saturday night, overcoming a double-digit deficit to beat Toledo on a three-pointer from Shammah Scott. Shammah Scott sends Akron to the NCAA Tournament! ONIONS!! pic.twitter.com/csZ6WDEEMH — Brian Rauf (@brauf33) March 15, 2026 This will be Akron's fourth NCAA Tournament appearance in the last five seasons, as John Groce has turned the Zips into the class of the conference since taking over prior to the 2017-18 season. Still, despite their success in the MAC, this program is still looking for its first-ever NCAA Tournament victory. Akron lost to Creighton by 17 points two years ago as a 14-seed and lost to Arizona by 28 points last season as a 13-seed. While Miami (OH)'s undefeated regular season was the biggest story in the MAC all season long — heck, it was the biggest story in the country — Akron's efficiency profile suggests the Zips have actually been the league's best team. Checking in at No. 64 on KenPom, Akron is the highest-ranked team from a traditional one-bid league. The RedHawks did beat Akron by three in Miami back on Jan. 3, yet the Zips have only lost one game since then (at Troy on Feb. 7). Their 10-game win streak is the fourth-longest active streak in the country, trailing only High Point, UMBC, and Duke. How has Akron gotten here, and what could make it a dangerous group in the NCAA Tournament? Elite three-point shooting Scott is one of several elite three-point shooters Akron has on its roster. He shoots 42.2 percent from long range on the season while Bowen Hardman, who was inserted into the starting lineup a month ago, is right at 42.0 percent. With freshman Eric Mahaffey shooting 40.3 percent off the bench, Akron has a trio of snipers hitting at least 40 percent of their attempts. And that's before we get to Tavari Johnson and Amani Lyles. Both of those first-team All-MAC players have started every game this season, though in very different roles. Johnson is the team's point guard and general engine while shooting 37.9 percent from three. He leads this elite offensive unit with 20.3 points per game while also leading the Zips with 5.0 assists. His quickne

result.text: Texas Tech vs Akron picks, predictions, odds for NCAA March Madness Skip to main content Home Schedule NFL News Draft Fantasy Odds Betting Newsletter USA TODAY Sports Texas Tech vs Akron picks, predictions, odds for March Madness game Jeremy Cluff Arizona Republic Show Caption Hide Caption March Madness Final Four predictions and national champion picks From chalk favorites to hot upsets, the panel shares Final Four picks and bold calls on who cuts down the nets. No. 5 seed Texas Tech and No. 12 seed Akron play Friday, March 20, in a March Madness NCAA Tournament first-round game . The Midwest Region game is scheduled for 9:40 a.m. MST and can be seen on truTV ( stream with Sling TV ). Who will win the March Madness game and advance to the second round in the NCAA Tournament bracket ? Check out these March Madness picks and March Madness predictions for the men's college basketball NCAA Tournament matchup . Michael McDaniel , Kevin Sweeney | Share on Facebook Share on X Add us as a preferred source Texas Tech star JT Toppin will miss the remainder of the season with a torn ACL. | Aryanna Frank-Imagn Images In this story: Texas Tech Red Raiders Fresh off a 78–75 overtime road win against then-No. 1 Arizona over the weekend, No. 13 Texas Tech was upset 72–67 on the road by Arizona State on Tuesday night in Tempe, Ariz. --> Fresh off a 78–75 overtime road win against then-No. 1 Arizona over the weekend, No. 13 Texas Tech was upset 72–67 on the road by Arizona State on Tuesday night in Tempe, Ariz. result.text: Is Texas Tech basketball's pre-March Madness losing skid troubling? Skip to main content Home Olympics NFL NCAAB NCAAW NBA Women's Sports NCAAF MLB WNBA Sports Betting Big Ten Sports SEC Sports Boxing Coaches Salaries Draft Hub Fantasy Football For The Win Formula 1 Golfweek High School Sports High School Sports Awards IndyCar MMA NASCAR National Signing Day NHL Soccer Sports Business Sports Pulse Sports Seriously Tennis WWE Youth Sports Outdoors Fantasy Bet With the People Clear Play Arizona Sports Indiana Sports Michigan Sports Ohio Sports Tennessee Sports Wisconsin Teams For Subscribers With 3 straight losses entering March Madness, is Texas Tech in trouble? Nathan Giese Lubbock Avalanche-Journal KANSAS CITY, Mo. — Nobody is going to pity Grant McCasland. He's handsomely paid to be the head coach of the Texas Tech basketball team at one of the most ambitious spending athletic departments in the country. But at the same time, you've gotta feel for the guy. McCasland has been the head coach at Tech for three full years now, spanning 103 total games . In that time, he's had his full arsenal available to him in just 16 games. Injuries have become second nature to McCasland that it seems he doesn't even flinch when another pops up. Half your rotation out for most of the year? Cool, let's set the program record for wins over Top 10 teams. Lose a national player of the year, Big 12 player of the year, and first team All-American candidate in one full swoop? Big whoop, we'll go into Ames and beat Iowa State. McCasland is an eternal optimist, always able to flip the negative into something deeper. It's part of what makes him such a great coach: finding joy in the struggle to reach the top of his profession. Even McCasland, though, has to think something's gotta give at some point. If it's not JT Toppin, having a historic season, tearing his ACL so close to the end of the season — after his team's bus tires were slashed after beating Arizona — it's seeing his other All-American, Christian Anderson, slipping and sliding all over the Big 12's insane ASP GlassFloor LED court , causing he and LeJuan Watts (perhaps an unrelated injury, perhaps related) to exit their Big 12 Tournament game with ailments. Compounding the current injury factor is the previous injuries to guys like Josiah Moseley, Luke Bamgboye and Marial Akuentok that set the team back from jump street. Before the season could even begin, the Red Raiders were shorthanded. It's the story of McCasland's existence at Texas Tech. Now is not the time to play the sympathy card, not that McCasland ever would. But he is a realist. As much as he wants to look at the bright side of things, he knows the Red Raiders and trending in the wrong direction and the worst possible time. Three straight losses to NCAA Tournament-bound teams isn't the end of the world. How they came about and what it says about his team's current state could be. The team has lacked poise under pressure, second-guessing every move th

result.text: Ticket Punched: Previewing the Akron Zips For the 2026 NCAA Tournament Articles Podcasts Scout Team Game Analysis Bracketology Women's Hoops Free Content Team Previews Scoreboard Search Sign Up Sign Up Create Your Account Get access to exclusive features and stay updated. Sign Up Sign Up Already have an account? Log In Articles Podcasts Scout Team Game Analysis Bracketology Women's Hoops Free Content Team Previews Scoreboard Become a member Become a member Home Articles & Editorials Ticket Punched: Previewing the Akron Zips For the 2026 NCAA Tournament Articles & Editorials Articles & Editorials Bracketology Bracketology Free Content Free Content Ticket Punched: Previewing the Akron Zips For the 2026 NCAA Tournament Featured Mar 15, 2026 by Brian Rauf No team in MAC history had ever won three conference tournaments in a row until Akron accomplished that feat on Saturday night, overcoming a double-digit deficit to beat Toledo on a three-pointer from Shammah Scott. Shammah Scott sends Akron to the NCAA Tournament! ONIONS!! pic.twitter.com/csZ6WDEEMH — Brian Rauf (@brauf33) March 15, 2026 This will be Akron's fourth NCAA Tournament appearance in the last five seasons, as John Groce has turned the Zips into the class of the conference since taking over prior to the 2017-18 season. Still, despite their success in the MAC, this program is still looking for its first-ever NCAA Tournament victory. Akron lost to Creighton by 17 points two years ago as a 14-seed and lost to Arizona by 28 points last season as a 13-seed. While Miami (OH)'s undefeated regular season was the biggest story in the MAC all season long — heck, it was the biggest story in the country — Akron's efficiency profile suggests the Zips have actually been the league's best team. Checking in at No. 64 on KenPom, Akron is the highest-ranked team from a traditional one-bid league. The RedHawks did beat Akron by three in Miami back on Jan. 3, yet the Zips have only lost one game since then (at Troy on Feb. 7). Their 10-game win streak is the fourth-longest active streak in the country, trailing only High Point, UMBC, and Duke. How has Akron gotten here, and what could make it a dangerous group in the NCAA Tournament? Elite three-point shooting Scott is one of several elite three-point shooters Akron has on its roster. He shoots 42.2 percent from long range on the season while Bowen Hardman, who was inserted into the starting lineup a month ago, is right at 42.0 percent. With freshman Eric Mahaffey shooting 40.3 percent off the bench, Akron has a trio of snipers hitting at least 40 percent of their attempts. And that's before we get to Tavari Johnson and Amani Lyles. Both of those first-team All-MAC players have started every game this season, though in very different roles. Johnson is the team's point guard and general engine while shooting 37.9 percent from three. He leads this elite offensive unit with 20.3 points per game while also leading the Zips with 5.0 assists. No. 5 seed Texas Tech and No. 12 seed Akron play Friday, March 20, in a March Madness NCAA Tournament first-round game . The Midwest Region game is scheduled for 9:40 a.m. MST and can be seen on truTV ( stream with Sling TV ). Who will win the March Madness game and advance to the second round in the NCAA Tournament bracket ? Check out these March Madness picks and March Madness predictions for the men's college basketball NCAA Tournament matchup . Texas Tech is an 8.5-point favorite over Akron in the game in March Madness odds provided by BetMGM Sportsbook . The Red Raiders are -325 on the moneyline. The Zips are +260. The over/under for the game is set at 156.5 points. Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me synthesize all the findings:

**Texas Tech (5-seed, 22-10):**
- KenPom #19, Torvik #12, AdjEM +25.19
- Elite offense: AdjO 125.1-126.3 (9th-12th nationally), solid defense: AdjD 98.8-99.9 (27th)
- **CRITICAL: Lost star JT Toppin (Big 12 Player of the Year) to torn ACL on Feb 17**
- Went 3-0 immediately after Toppin's injury but then lost 3 straight entering the tournament
- Christian Anderson (groin injury from Big 12 Tournament) cleared to play
- 8.5-point favorite despite injuries
- Big 12 schedule strength is elite

**Akron (12-seed, 29-5):**
- KenPom #64, Torvik #68, AdjEM +13.02
- AdjO 117.4-119.1 (52nd-60th), AdjD 105.2-106.1 (99th)
- Won 3 consecutive MAC Tournament titles (historic)
- Led by veteran guards Shammah Scott and Tavari Johnson
- Tournament Index grades them as 20th-strongest 12-seed out of 52 in last 13 tournaments (weak for a 12)
- Projects only 0.33 wins given seed and team strength

**Key Analysis:**
- Even without Toppin, Texas Tech's remaining metrics are significantly better than Akron's (~12-point efficiency margin gap)
- The 5/12 line historically produces upsets ~35% of the time, but this Akron team is rated as a weak 12-seed
- Texas Tech's 3-game losing streak and injury concerns are real, but the talent gap remains substantial
- The 8.5-point spread suggests the market still sees Texas Tech as a comfortable favorite despite Toppin's absence

Akron's veteran-led squad won three consecutive MAC Tournament titles and brings a 29-5 record, but the Tournament Index grades them as one of the weaker 12-seeds in recent history, and their defense (99th-ranked AdjD of 105-106) will struggle to contain Texas Tech's Big 12-caliber scoring even without Toppin. The 8.5-point spread confirms the market still views Texas Tech as a comfortable favorite, and while the 5/12 seed line is historically upset-prone and Texas Tech's current form is concerning, the talent and efficiency gap is too wide for Akron to overcome absent a truly exceptional shooting performance."
